coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemical paper-based biosensor for microRNA via strand displacement reaction and metal-organic frameworks
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, He
Jian, Yannan
Kong, Qingkun
Liu, Haiyun
Lan, Feifei
Liang, Linlin
Ge, Shenguang
Yu, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: A novel electrochemical biosensor based on hairpin assembly target recycling for signal amplification and Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) modified Cu-based metal-organic frameworks (Cu-MOFs) for catalysis was constructed for highly sensitive detection of microRNA (miRNA). It was worth noting that the recycling of target was finished only based on strand displacement reaction without nuclease. In addition, a novel catalytic material of Cu-MOFs was prepared, which could provide more large surface area for AuNPs enveloping and DNA strand 1 (S1) immobilization. In this sensing process, the released target was available for initiating many of cycles, and the prepared S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs was linked to the modified electrode by chain hybridization, which was promising for signal amplification. All electrical signal measurements and chain displacement reactions are carried out on a compatibly designed origami electrochemical device (OECD). In the presence of glucose, AuNPs and Cu-MOFs cooperatively catalyzed the oxidation of glucose, resulting in the wide linear detection range from 1.0fM to 10nM and the detection limit of 0.35fM for miRNA-155 with extremely high sensitivity. As we expect, the newly designed biosensor was successfully applied to the detection of miRNA-155 in serum samples from healthy people to cancer patients. Therefore, this proposed method could provide a promising and safe pathway for sensitive detection of miRNAs in clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MicroRNA
Origami electrochemical device
Signal amplification
Cu-based metal-organic frameworks
Stand displacement reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the miRNA-155 electrochemical biosensor
Characterization of Au-PWE, Cu-MOFs and AuNPs@Cu-MOFs
Feasibility of the proposed electrochemical biosensor
Reproducibility, specificity and stability of the proposed electrochemical biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication of the biosensor: (A) preparation procedure of S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs; (B) the detection principle for glucose and the strategy of signal amplification. (C) structure of H1, target, and H2.

(A) SEM images of grown Au on the surface of paper fibers in PWE (inset Fig.: magnified SEM images of Au-PWE); (B) SEM images, (C) TEM image and (D) IR analysis of Cu-MOFs; (E) SEM image and (F) EDS analysis of AuNPs@Cu-MOFs.

(A) CV and (B) EIS of electrodes with different modified in PBS (pH 7.0, 0.1M) including 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as a probe of redox: (a) unmodified bare PWE, (b) Au-PWE, (c) H1/Au-PWE, (d) MCH/H1/Au-PWE, (e) H2/miRNA-155/MCH/H1/Au-PWE (f) S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs/H2/MCH/H1/Au-PWE.

Optimization of experimental parameters by the proposed biosensor incubated with 10nM miRNA-155 and detected in pH 7.0 PBS: (A) Influence of the concentrations of glucose on the biosensor; (B) effect of hybridization time between capture probes and S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs; (C) influence of the reaction time for cycle.

DPV responses of the as-prepared miRNA biosensor before (a) and after (b) catalysis, according to different catalytic strategies: (A) catalysis of Cu-MOFs; (B) cooperative catalysis of AuNPs and Cu-MOFs. (A) and (B) were detected in 2mL of PBS (0.1M, pH 7.0) including 240μL of 5.0mM glucose.

(A) DPV responses of the biosensor with different miRNA-155 concentrations in 2mL of PBS (0.1M, pH 7.0) including 240μL of 5.0mM glucose: (a) 1fM, (b) 10fM, (c) 100fM, (d) 1pM, (e) 10pM, (f) 100pM, (g) 1nM, (h) 10nM, (i) 100nM, (j) 1μM, (k) 10μM, (l) 100μM, (m) 1mM. (B) The curve of DPV peak current vs the logarithm of miRNA-155 concentration (1fM to 1mM); inset Figure: the calibration curve of DPV peak current vs the logarithm of miRNA-155 concentration (1fM to 10nM).

(A) The specificity for different disturbances of this proposed miRNA biosensor: (a) blank solution (0fM miRNA-155), (b) nDNA (10pM), (c) nRNA (10pM), (d) tDNA (10pM), (e) sRNA (10pM), (f) miRNA-155 (1pM). (B) The stability of the proposed electrochemical biosensor at room temperature (a), in a refrigerator (b) and in a freezer (c).
Performance of miRNA detection contrasted with other works.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: FL: fluorescence; CA: chronoamperometry; DPV: differential pulse voltammetry; SPR: surface plasmon resonance; ECL: electrochemiluminescence.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: GE: gold electrodes; GCE: glassy carbon electrode; PWE: paper working electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: GQDs: graphene quantum dots; AuNCs: Au nanoclusters; GO: graphene oxide; NFs: nanoflowers.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of miRNA-155 spiked with different concentrations of miRNA in human serum (n=3) by using the proposed biosensor.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemical paper-based biosensor for microRNA via strand displacement reaction and metal-organic frameworks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Interdisciplinary Research, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel electrochemical biosensor based on hairpin assembly target recycling for signal amplification and Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) modified Cu-based metal-organic frameworks (Cu-MOFs) for catalysis was constructed for highly sensitive detection of microRNA (miRNA). It was worth noting that the recycling of target was finished only based on strand displacement reaction without nuclease. In addition, a novel catalytic material of Cu-MOFs was prepared, which could provide more large surface area for AuNPs enveloping and DNA strand 1 (S1) immobilization. In this sensing process, the released target was available for initiating many of cycles, and the prepared S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs was linked to the modified electrode by chain hybridization, which was promising for signal amplification. All electrical signal measurements and chain displacement reactions are carried out on a compatibly designed origami electrochemical device (OECD). In the presence of glucose, AuNPs and Cu-MOFs cooperatively catalyzed the oxidation of glucose, resulting in the wide linear detection range from 1.0fM to 10nM and the detection limit of 0.35fM for miRNA-155 with extremely high sensitivity. As we expect, the newly designed biosensor was successfully applied to the detection of miRNA-155 in serum samples from healthy people to cancer patients. Therefore, this proposed method could provide a promising and safe pathway for sensitive detection of miRNAs in clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs), a class of short single-stranded noncoding RNAs (∼18-25 nucleotides) [1], have aroused intense interest as drug discovery targets or clinically important biomarkers for cancers [2] (liver, pancreas, esophagus, skin etc.) and other diseases (diabetes, leukemia, cardiovascular disease etc.) [3]. The accurate and sensitive detection of miRNAs is a forward-looking desire, which is conductive to acquaintance the biological processes related with the disease [4,5].

There are a lot of methods for detection of miRNA such as fluorescence [6], electrochemistry [7], colorimetry [8], microanalysis [9,10] and so on. Recently, electrochemical biosensors have great prospects because of the advantages of fast response time, high sensitivity and low cost [11–14]. Up to now, a wide variety of electrochemical miRNA biosensors have been manufactured [15,16]. Since the low content in the biological samples of miRNA, the desire to improve the detection sensitivity by using various amplification strategies [17,18] is quite intense. On the one hand, a number of DNA-based signal amplification strategies [19] for enhance the detection signal have been reported. On the other hand, many electrochemical methods of nucleic acid detection are concentrated on using nanostructured conductive materials as a support matrix to improve the sensitivity of the sensors [20,21]. Briefly, amplification of electrochemical signal in our work can be achieved by target recycle processes, nonenzymatic catalytic reactions and nanomaterials [19].

Due to the fact that paper is a biocompatible porous cellulose fiber web with large surface area and porous nature, paper-based analytical devices also stand for new approaches to make low-cost devices, simple, disposable and portable for molecular analysis, health and environmental monitoring. Efforts have been made to improve the immobilization of bio-recognition substance in paper as well as the signal amplification technique for paper-based assays [2,22]. Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) have attracted much attention in different bio-affinity assays due to their unique physical and chemical properties, such as superior conductivity, long-term stability, large surface area and biocompatibility [23,24]. In view of the above advantages, Au-paper working electrode (Au-PWE) is fabricated on a compatibly designed origami electrochemical device (OECD) through the growth of an interconnected Au layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in the paper sample zone to enhance the conductivity of the paper sample zone. Due to high surface area, porous structure of paper and high effective surface area of the resulting Au layer on the cellulose fibers, the active surface area of the Au-PWE for immobilizing the recognition elements is much higher than that of the unmodified PWE.

Recently, nanomaterials [25,26] have been widely applied in biosensing on account to their large surface area resulted from the porous construction which can load a large number of molecules and accelerate the rate of diffusion. Therefore, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are ingeniously introduced in our system. In general, MOFs are a large family of materials with organic ligands as linkers and metal ions as nodes [27], which can form three-dimensional frameworks with unique properties containing high pore volume, large surface area, and extraordinary thermal stability [28]. With these excellent properties, MOFs have been applied in gas storage, separation, drug delivery, and chemical sensing [29,30]. Structural diversification of MOFs with other functional materials demonstrate many preponderances on account of their synergistic effect compared with pure MOFs [31,32]. Among them, Cu-MOFs have received special concerns in electrochemical applications because of their well-defined configuration, unique electric conductivity, and superior catalytic activity [33,34]. Besides, previous work has shown that Cu-MOFs can be used to construct a biosensor based on their catalytic activity for the detection of small molecules [35] such as glucose and H2O2.

Inspired by this perspective, target triggered cycle as signal amplification strategy and Cu-MOFs as catalyst were introduced in the proposed biosensor [36]. AuNPs-functionalized nanoparticles Cu-MOFs material (AuNPs@Cu-MOFs) were synthesized in this work, which were not only served as nanocarriers to immobilize a large number of DNA strand 1 (S1) and as electroactive materials for signal reporting but also acted as a catalyst to oxidize glucose. In addition, AuNPs [37] had been synthesized in this article, which had unique biocompatibility, excellent conductivity and significantly increased specific surface area. It was worth noting that this hybrid material not only enhanced the sensibility of electrochemical biosensors toward glucose, but also retained the copper catalytic activities of Cu-MOFs.

Herein, an ultrasensitive electrochemical biosensor was designed for the detection of miRNA-155. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the Au was grown in order to enhance the conductivity of paper electrode and immobilize hairpin probe H1. The hairpin structure of H1 was opened with the presence of miRNA-155 (target). The H2 that had more combining capacity with H1 than miRNA-155 hybridized with H1 liberating target. Then the freed target was used for starting another cycle. After the cyclic process completed, a great deal of H1-H2 duplex was fabricated [38,39]. The exposed region of H1-H2 duplex could combine with S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs. Therefore, the catalytic amplification of miRNA biosensors was resoundingly fabricated based on AuNPs and Cu-MOFs. The present work thus demonstrated the promising applications of AuNPs and Cu-MOFs in the development of highly sensitive, cost-effective and environmentally friendly electrochemical biosensors. The proposed detection method of miRNA-155 opened up a hopeful platform for clinical assays of other biomolecules.

Gold chloride (HAuCl4) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, U.S.A.). Polyvinylpyrrolidone K30 (PVP), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and glucose were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). 2-Amino terephthalic acid (NH2–BDC) and copper nitrate-trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chem. Re. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Trishydroxy-methylaminomethane hydrochloride (Tris-HCl) and 6-Mercapto-1-hexanol (98%, MCH) were obtained from dojindo china Ltd. All chemicals were used as received and reagent grade without further purification. Whatman chromatography paper ♯1 (58.0cm 68.0cm) was purchased from GE Healthcare Worldwide (Shanghai, China) adjust the size (A4 size) before further use. All of RNA and DNA sequences (see Supporting Information S1) were purified and synthesized from Sangon (Shanghai, China).

The buffer to participate in this work was as follows: DNA hybridization buffer (HB1): 10mM Tris-HCl, 1.0mM EDTA, 1.0M NaCl (pH 7.0), miRNA hybridization buffer (HB2): 50mM Tris-HCl, 100mM NaCl, 10mM MgCl2 (pH 7.5). Working buffer: 0.1M phosphate buffered solution (PBS, pH 7.0) was consist of 2.0mM MgCl2, 10mM NaH2PO4 and 10mM Na2HPO4. All solutions were freshly prepared using ultrapure water (resistivity≥18MΩcm) as a solvent.

All electrochemical measurements, including differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were performed on CHI 760D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments Inc., Shanghai of China). The IR-vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-3101 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded on a JEOL JSM-5510 scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with a JEOL 4000 EX microscope. An OECD consisted of three screen printed electrodes (working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode), was prepared from our group (see Supporting Information S2).

Cu-MOFs were prepared according to the literature with some revision [33,40]. First, a mixture solution which contained 4mL ethanol, 4mL DMF and 0.20g PVP was prepared. Subsequently, 4mL of DMF containing 18.1mg Cu(NO3)2 and 5.4mg 2-amino terephthalic acid (NH2−BDC) were added to the previous solution and ultrasonicated for 30min. Next, the solution was heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 100°C for 5h. After that, 20mL of DMF was added to dissolve the obtained precipitate, and the solution was further heated to 8h at 100°C to remove the unreacted reagents. Finally, the obtained Cu-MOFs were collected by centrifugal separation and dispersed in deionized water (2mL) for further use.

AuNPs@Cu-MOFs were obtained by dropping 20μL of 1% HAuCl4 and 2mL of 2mM NaBH4 into the above Cu-MOFs solution and stirring for 1h. After centrifugated and washed, AuNPs@Cu-MOFs were collected. Next, 200μL of 2μM S1 was added in the solution of AuNPs@Cu-MOFs by mixing for 12h at 4°C, and the S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs was obtained by the strong interaction between the AuNPs and −NH2 groups of S1. The unconjugated S1 was removed by centrifugation and washing, and then the obtained S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs were dispersed in deionized water (2mL) and stored in 4°C. The process of preparation for the signal probes of S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs was illustrated in Scheme 1A.

Au layer was built up on the surface of cellulosic fiber with the aid of Au seeds [41–43] in the PWE. And an OECD was fabricated based on novel Au-PWE. Because of the strong conductivity of Au and perforated weave of chromatography paper, the sensitivity and electrochemistry surface area of the Au-PWE was better than that of the ungroomed PWE [37,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the miRNA-155 electrochemical biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The solution of all the hairpin oligonucleotides were heated to 95°C, annealed for 2min, then slowly cooling to room temperature before using. First of all, 10μL of H1 (2μM) was added to the surface of the Au-PWE layer at room temperature for 16h, in order to assemble H1 on the Au-PWE. Subsequently, the modified electrode was rinsed with ultrapure water and blocked with MCH (1.0mM, 10μL) of 30min in order to avoid nonspecific adsorption. Immediately, 10μL of mixture solution including H2 (2μM) and different concentrations of miRNA-155 (target) were added dropwise to the modified electrode and then incubated at 37°C for 120min. Next, the solution of S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs (10μL) was added to the surface of modified electrode and incubated at 4°C for 120min after rinsed with ultrapure water. Then modified electrode was washed, dried, and stored at 4°C. At last, the biosensor could measure electrochemical signal and provide the corresponding quantitative criteria for miRNA detection.

After the target chain cycles were performed, the as-modified electrode was rinsed with deionized water to get rid of the unbound reagents, and then 10μL of S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs was dropped into the electrode for 120min. DPV measurements were performed in 2mL of PBS (pH 7.0, 0.1M) including 240μL of glucose at room temperature and the potential range was from −0.3V to 0.4V, 50ms pulse width, sweeping rate 50mVs−1 and 0.2s pulse period. In this case, Cu-MOFs can catalyze glucose to gluconolactone, resulting in an enormous amplified electrochemical signal. DPV response of the electrode (with and without the preparation of target) was compared and illustrated in Scheme 1B.

As can be seen from Scheme 1, the generalized principle of the catalytic amplification of the biosensor for miRNA-155 detection was illustrated, and this principle could be obtained from gel electrophoretic image (see Supporting Information S3). Two hairpin DNA fragments (H2 and H1) were used in the process of biosensor. Under the absence of target, they maintained the structure of stabilization sufficiently and couldn't automatically hybridize with each other. To begin with, the hairpin DNA H1 was combined with Au on the surface of PWE by AuS bond [15]. After the target was added, it could open H1 stem-loop structure and make the complementary sequence of H1 exposed and hybridized with H2 to form dsDNA. With the introduction of H2, the binding of H2 and H1 occurred according to a process of branch migration. Since the H1-H2 duplex was more stable and longer than the H1-T hybrids, the H2 would displace and release the target when it hybridized with H1. The released target could be used for the next hybridization cycle with H2. After that, each miRNA-155 (target) might undergo many cycles [45,46], which led to combine with many of H2. The remainder single strand fragment of H2 could be further hybridized with S1, which was functionalized on AuNPs@Cu-MOFs. According to the previous work [47], the possible catalytic reaction of glucose oxidation by Cu-MOFs might be as follows: Cu (II)-MOFs were first oxidized to Cu (III)-MOFs in higher potential, and then Cu (III)-MOFs could oxidize glucose to gluconolactone, resulting in the regeneration of Cu (II)-MOFs. And this process would result in a huge amplification of electrochemical signal. Thus, the sensitivity of the novel miRNA biosensor could be significantly improved by using both Cu-MOFs as catalysis and miRNA target as a strand displacement reaction inductor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Au-PWE, Cu-MOFs and AuNPs@Cu-MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by SEM, TEM and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). Au-PWE was prepared in a growth solution under the autocatalytic reduction mechanism. As show in Fig. 1 A, a dense conductive and continuous Au layer with interconnected Au was completely covered on the surface of paper fibers when Au grown up. In Fig. 1B, the SEM images of Cu-MOFs which was like-flower. Fig. 1C and D exhibited the TEM images and FT-IR spectrum (see Supporting Information S4) of Cu-MOFs, which showed the particle size of Cu-MOFs (150nm) and contained −NH2 groups. Cu-MOFs displayed well-formed spheres which consisted of single-layer nanosheets with large surface area. In addition, the background of Cu-MOFs was clear without other impurities, suggesting that Cu-MOFs owned a stable structure which was suitable for biosensor construction.

The analysis of elemental and material morphology of AuNPs@Cu-MOFs was also characterized with SEM and EDS. Fig. 1E exhibited the SEM images of Cu-MOFs@AuNPs. In Fig. 1F, the characteristic peaks of Au and Cu were obviously observed in the EDS spectrum of AuNPs@Cu-MOFs obtained. With AuNPs produced, it could be seen that Cu-MOFs were surrounded by many light nanoparticles which were assigned to AuNPs, illustrating AuNPs were adsorbed to the external of Cu-MOFs by chemical interaction between −NH2 of Cu-MOFs and AuNPs [48]. On the basis of this finding it could be inferred that divalent Cu ions acted as nodes to combine with organic ligands forming Cu-MOFs. Thus, the SEM and EDS results give the proof to the characterization of the elemental composition of AuNPs@Cu-MOFs.

CV and EIS are powerful and facile electrochemical techniques to demonstrate the manufacture of the electrochemical biosensor [49,50]. The CV of the electrode modified was performed in PBS (pH 7.0, 0.1M) containing 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. As we could see from Fig. 2 A, the bare PWE illustrated a pair of clearly defined redox peaks (curve a). After Au particle was dropped onto the PWE, the redox peak current (curve b) increased because Au could accelerate electron transfer. Afterward, with the combination of H1, the decrease of peak current (curve c) was achieved, accounting for the fact that the effect of inhibition between H1 and ferricyanide. After the modified electrode was blocked with non-conductive MCH, the redox peak current decreased (curve d). Subsequently, the redox peak current was further reduced (curve e) after incubation of the mixture solution of H2 and target (10pM), which was showed loading more non-conducting DNA strands on the surface of electrode and inhibited the spread of redox probe. Finally, the redox peak current was further reduced after immobilizing S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs on modified electrode (curve f), due to the increase of steric hindrance. These results demonstrated that the assembly process was very successful.

EIS was also commonly used to study the interface properties of modified electrode. Generally, the charge transfer resistance (Ret) corresponded to the semicircular diameter of the higher parts of the Nyquist diagram, and the variability of Ret was strongly related to the different electrode modifications. Fig. 2B showed the EIS of the modified electrode. The bare PWE showed a small semicircle (curve a). After Au particle was dropped onto the electrode, Ret reduced obviously (curve b) due to the conductivity of Au. Afterward, Ret increased (curve c) when H1 was immobilized onto the Au-PWE. Moreover, the Ret kept increasing (curve d) after the modified electrode was blocked with non-conductive MCH, which was attributed to the MCH defined the interfacial electron transfer. After H2 was obtained with the help of miRNA-155, it could be seen that Ret increased (curve e) because the hybridization of H2 and H1 further hinder the electron trasfer. At last, Ret further increased after S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs were binded with H2 (curve f), due to the increase of steric hindrance.

In order to achieve optimal sensing performance, the important experimental parameters such as the concentration of glucose, the hybridization time between H2 and S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs and the reaction time for cycle to the response of biosensor were investigated with 10nM miRNA-155 as a model. The signal of biosensor was directly related to the concentration of glucose and then the concentration of glucose in 2mL of PBS (0.1M, pH 7.0) was evaluated from 0mM to 8.0mM. Reaction time for cycle and hybridization time between H2 and S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs were set for 120min. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the catalysis peak current of Cu-MOFs increased with the increase of glucose concentration from 0 to 5.0mM, and then the catalysis peak current remained constant. Thus, 5.0mM was chosen as the optimized concentration of glucose.

The reaction time for cycle was studied from 40 to 120min, the other conditions were the hybridization time between H2 and S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs of 120min and the glucose concentration in PBS (0.1M, pH 7.0) of 5.0mM. Fig. 3B showed that the catalysis peak current of Cu-MOFs increased with the increase of reaction time for cycle. It could be found the peak current change trends of Cu-MOFs tended to be stable until the reaction time was 120min, which indicated that the optimized reaction time for cycle was 120min.

The effect of the hybridization time between capture probes and S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs was also investigated by ranging the hybridization time from 40 to 120min, the other conditions were immobilized (reaction time for cycle of 120min and the glucose concentration in PBS buffer of 5.0mM). As shown in Fig. 3C, the catalysis peak current of Cu-MOFs increased rapidly with the increase of the hybridization time because more S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs were introduced onto the electrode surface. The maximum catalysis peak current of Cu-MOFs was observed when the hybridization time was maintained for 120min, and then it stayed constant. Thus 120min was selected as the optimal hybridization time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the proposed electrochemical biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experiment conditions, Fig. 5 illustrated that the analytical performance of the proposed biosensor for the detection of miRNA-155. As we could see from Fig. 5A, the DPV signal of the biosensor [14,18] increased with the increasing miRNA-155 concentrations in PBS (pH 7.0, 0.1M) including 240μL of glucose. Fig. 5B illustrated that the catalytic peak current of miRNA-155 increased with increasing of miRNA-155 concentrations from 1fM to 10nM and then the catalytic peak current remained constant. The calibration chart for quantitatively determining miRNA-155 was shown in the inset figure of Fig. 5B. The DPV responses had good linear relationships with the logarithm of the concentrations of miRNA-155 ranges from 1fM to 10nM. The coefficient of linear correlation was 0.9967 and the detection limit was 0.35fM (defined as signal-to-noise ratio of three times). It was reasonable to believe that the proposed biosensor was effective for the detection of miRNA-155. Table 1 showed that the performance of miRNA detection contrasted with other works. As we expected, the detection limit and linear range of this proposed biosensor was better than other reported methods. Under the same method, the sensitivity of this biosensor was enhanced, which was mainly attributed to the Au-PWE with good electroconductivity and AuNPs@Cu-MOFs as catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, specificity and stability of the proposed electrochemical biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of the assay were evaluated by using the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the intra-assays and inter-assays [21]. Three different concentrations of miRNA solution (including 10.0fM, 10.0pM and 10.0nM miRNA) were used as examples, and each RSD represented the average value of five assays on the same batch of OECD. It could be gained from experimental results that the RSD values of the intra-assay were 5.63%, 4.35%, and 3.97% at 10.0fM, 10.0pM and 10.0nM miRNA, whereas the RSD values of the inter-assay using different batches of OECD were 3.81%, 5.62%, and 4.96% at 10.0fM, 10.0pM and 10.0nM miRNA, respectively. This consequence indicated that the reproducibility of the electrochemical biosensor was accepted.

The specificity of the proposed electrochemical biosensor [3] was evaluated by incubation with single base differential miRNA (sRNA, 10pM), non-complementary miRNA (nRNA, 10pM), non-complementary DNA (nDNA, 10pM), three-base mismatch miRNA (tRNA) and the fully complementary sequences (miRNA, 1pM). The proposed biosensor illustrated the negligible interference with sRNA, nRNA, tRNA, nDNA and by using an excess (10-fold) amount of non-target analytes (Fig. 6 A). This indicated that the specificity of the electrochemical biosensor was acceptable.

To investigate the stability [46,57], the proposed sensing platform was dried and stored under optimized conditions at room temperature (25°C), in a refrigerator (4°C) and in a freezer (−20°C). Fig. 6B showed the average storage stability under three conditions over a period of 30days. MiRNA-155 (10nM) was used for each test and each experiment was performed in triplicate. The stability of the biosensor stored at room temperature was 96% of the initial response in the first 10days and then decreased to 80.66% in the next 20days. A remarkable average stability of up to 96.08% and 95.23% of the initial response was retained after 30days of storage in the refrigerator and freezer. This indicated that the optimized reaction temperature for electrode was 4°C. The results demonstrate the excellent storage stability of the biosensor.

Spiked experiments were achieved by adding different concentrations of miRNA-155 to 10-fold diluted healthy human serum (obtained from Cancer Research Center of Shandong Tumor Hospital), with the purpose of detect the reliability of proposed biosensor. As illustrated in Table 2 , the RSD was ranging from 2.81% to 6.33% and the recovery was ranging from 93.51% to 100.4%, indicating that the proposed biosensor could be used to determine of miRNA-155 in real biological sample.

In summary, a paper-based electrochemical biosensor was achieved for the detection of miRNA-155 by combining target chain replacement with synergetic catalysis of AuNPs and Cu-MOF. It is worth pointing that the S1-AuNPs@Cu-MOFs signal probe was linked on the as-prepared electrode by hybridizing with capture probes (H2). AuNPs@Cu-MOFs are not only used as nanocarriers to immobilize S1 and as electroactive materials for signal reporting, but also performed as catalyst to oxidize glucose for signal amplification with target recycling. This novel design of the biosensor provided a relatively low detection limit of 0.35fM and a sensitive detection from 1fM to 10nM for miRNA-155. Compared with the other electrochemical detection methods, the biosensor illustrated a satisfying sensitivity detection and a wide range of linearity for miRNA-155. Therefore, this principle provide a pathway for miRNA detection in clinical applications and open up new opportunities for other analyte detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575051, 21775055, 21501090, 21475052), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (2017C01-3), and the program of Taishan Scholars.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.10.188.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Screening of pre-miRNA-155 binding peptides for apoptosis inducing activity using peptide microarrays

In situ DNA-templated synthesis of silver nanoclusters for ultrasensitive and label-free electrochemical detection of microRNA

Signal-on electrochemical detection of antibiotics at zeptomole level based on target-aptamer binding triggered multiple recycling amplification
Ultrasensitive, colorimetric detection of microRNAs based on isothermal exponential amplification reaction-assisted gold nanoparticle amplification
Microarray-based, high-throughput gene expression profiling of microRNAs
Prognostic impact of miR-155 in non-small cell lung cancer evaluated by in situ hybridization
Paper-based device for colorimetric and photoelectrochemical quantification of the flux of H2O2 releasing from MCF-7 cancer cells

A highly sensitive and selective electrochemical biosensor for direct detection of microRNAs in serum
Label-free and enzyme-free homogeneous electrochemical biosensing strategy based on hybridization chain reaction: a facile, sensitive, and highly specific MicroRNA assay
Magnetic zirconium hexacyanoferrate(II) nanoparticle as tracing tag for electrochemical DNA assay
All-graphene composite materials for signal amplification toward ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensing of tumor marker
Silver nanoclusters for high-efficiency quenching of CdS nanocrystal electrochemiluminescence and sensitive detection of microRNA
Ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of microRNA based on an arched probe mediated isothermal exponential amplification

Microfluidic paper-based analytical device for photoelectrochemical immunoassay with multiplex signal amplification using multibranched hybridization chain reaction and PdAu enzyme mimetics
Growth of gold-manganese oxide nanostructures on a 3D origami device for glucose-oxidase label based electrochemical immunosensor
Paper analytical devices for dynamic evaluation of cell surface N-glycan expression via a bimodal biosensor based on multibranched hybridization chain reaction amplification
The use of tetraethyl orthosilicate silane (TEOS) for surface-treatment of hardened cement-based materials: a comparison study with normal treatment agents
A precursor-derived morphology-controlled synthesis method for mesoporous Co3O4 nanostructures towards supercapacitor application
Synthetic access to atomically dispersed metals in metal-organic frameworks via a combined atomic-layer-deposition-in-MOF and metal-exchange approach
Semiconductor@metal-organic framework core-shell heterostructures: a case of ZnO@ZIF-8 nanorods with selective photoelectrochemical response

Pd@Cu(II)-MOF-catalyzed aerobic oxidation of benzylic alcohols in air with high conversion and selectivity

Post-synthesis modification of a metal-organic framework to form metallosalen-containing MOF materials
A porous Cu(II)-MOF with proline embellished cavity: cooperative catalysis for the Baylis-Hillman reaction
Molecularly imprinted polymer grafted porous Au-paper electrode for an microfluidic electro-analytical origami device
Electrochemiluminescent graphene quantum dots as a sensing platform: a dual amplification for MicroRNA assay
Signal-off electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on phi29 DNA polymerase mediated strand displacement amplification for MicroRNA detection

Gold-silver nanocomposite-functionalized graphene based electrochemiluminescence immunosensor using graphene quantum dots coated porous PtPd nanochains as labels
An origami electrochemiluminescence immunosensor based on gold/graphene for specific, sensitive point-of-care testing of carcinoembryonic antigen
Real-time and in situ enzyme inhibition assay for the flux of hydrogen sulfide based on 3D interwoven AuPd-reduced graphene oxide network
Off to on surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy platform with padlock probe-based exponential rolling circle amplification for ultrasensitive detection of MicroRNA 155
In situ electrochemical generation of electrochemiluminescent silver naonoclusters on target-Cycling synchronized rolling circle amplification platform for MicroRNA detection
A green strategy to prepare metal oxide superstructure from metal-organic frameworks
An aptasensor for sensitive detection of human breast cancer cells by using porous GO/Au composites and porous PtFe alloy as effective sensing platform and signal amplification labels
Graphene-assisted label-free homogeneous electrochemical biosensing strategy based on aptamer-switched bidirectional DNA polymerization
In situ assembly of porous Au-paper electrode and functionalization of magnetic silica nanoparticles with HRP via click chemistry for microcystin-LR immunoassay
A sensitive electrochemical biosensor for microRNA detection based on streptavidin-gold nanoparticles and enzymatic amplification

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: He Wang studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Yannan Jian studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Qingkun Kong studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.

Haiyun Liu obtained his PhD degree in Chemical Engineering and Materials Science from Shandong Normal University in 2014. He then joined the faculty at University of Jinan, where currently he is a lecturer of chemistry. His current scientific interests are focused on nucleic acid DNA-based biosensor, bioimaging and biochemical analysis.
Feifei Lan studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Linlin Liang studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.

Shenguang Ge is currently an associate professor at University of Jinan. He received his B.S. and M.S. degrees from the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan in 2003 and 2006, respectively, and obtained his Ph.D. degree in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University in 2013. He joined the University of Jinan in 2006, and he is working on the design and synthesis of functional materials, as well as their applications for biosensors and bioanalysis.

Jinghua Yu received her PhD degree in analytical chemistry in 2003 from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, China. She is currently positioned as a professor at University of Jinan. She spends most of her time investigating biomedical engineering, especially for the development of biosensor devices and analytical tools.
